teamObjective.explanation: As the opening leader with only low non-trump cards, the immediate team objective is to start the trick cheaply and either win it or let partner take control, preserving any marginal high-card value for later.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) likely holds a random 3-card hand with unknown strength; no signal has been sent yet, so partner is equally likely to be strong or weak in the current suit.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents (players 1 and 3) each hold 3 unknown cards; with no prior play, they are likely to contain a mix of ranks and suits, and either opponent may be able to beat a low spade lead.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the lowest card (3 of spades) expends minimum strength, keeps the 4-5-6 sequence intact for possible later use, and gives partner the cheapest opportunity to win or signal.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 6 is the highest card in hand and the closest to being a winner; playing it first wastes the only card with any realistic chance of taking a trick uncontested.
The 5 is still the second-best card and more valuable than the 3 or 4 for a later trick; leading it now sacrifices more potential without compensating benefit.
The 4 is only slightly higher than the 3 but still loses to almost any non-trump or trump, so keeping it preserves a small escalation option.

risks: An opponent may have a higher spade or any trump and immediately take the lead.
If partner cannot beat the lead, the team loses this trick and opponent may lead a pattern we cannot match.
All four cards are non-trump, so any trump played beats our lead.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the 3, we minimize the information revealed and the strength committed, leaving the slightly higher spades to respond to future leads or to attempt a later trick.

notes: Opening lead with four consecutive low spades; no trump or bomb available, so a minimal single lead is the standard opening.
